Title: Trump says Iran 'collapsing financially' over Hormuz closure
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said Tuesday that Iran was "collapsing financially" over the closure of the vital Strait of Hormuz.
"Iran is collapsing financially! They want the Strait of Hormuz opened immediately," he wrote on Truth Social, adding that the Islamic republic was "starving for cash."
Trump posted earlier that Teheran said it supported the closure of the strait to "save face" under the US blockade of Iranian ports.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: HSA clarifies woman in viral ward deposit incident not a staff member
Article:
JOHOR BARU: The Sultanah Aminah Hospital (HSA) management has clarified that a woman involved in a viral incident, where she allegedly posed as a staff member to collect ward deposits, is not an employee.
HSA, in a post on Threads, said it took a serious view of the allegation involving an individual requesting cash payments for ward deposits without issuing official receipts.
Initial investigations confirmed the individual was not a hospital staff member, and she has been handed over to the police for further action.
The public is advised to remain vigilant and ensure all payments are made only at official revenue counters, where valid receipts must be issued for every transaction.
This is to prevent them from falling victim to scams by irresponsible parties attempting to take advantage, the post read.
Earlier, a woman claimed her mother was asked to pay RM30 in cash to the individual when registering her father's admission to a ward. No receipt was provided during the encounter.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Capital market reforms on track under five-year masterplan amid uncertainties
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's capital market is on track to deliver reforms, strengthen competitiveness and enhance investor protection under the Capital Market Masterplan 2026–2030.
Securities Commission (SC) chairman Datuk Mohammad Faiz Azmi said the plan comes at a time of heightened global uncertainty, with geopolitical tensions and technological disruption reshaping market dynamics.
"With the masterplan in place, SC is optimistic the capital market will stay on course in delivering reforms, boosting competitiveness and inclusiveness, strengthening governance frameworks, and enhancing investor protection," he said at the release of the SC Annual Report 2025 today.
Malaysia's capital market expanded 3.2 per cent to a record RM4.3 trillion in 2025, from RM4.2 trillion a year earlier, supported by stronger corporate bond issuances and higher fund management inflows.
Mohammad Faiz said the performance underscored the market's resilience despite a challenging external environment.
"The 2025 results reflect the fundamental strength of the Malaysian capital market.
"By balancing market development with safeguarding market integrity, the SC continues to provide a stable and innovative environment for all stakeholders," he added.
Average daily trading value in the equity market fell 19.7 per cent to RM2.76 billion in 2025, from RM3.44 billion in 2024, reflecting cautious investor sentiment.
The Islamic capital market remained robust, growing 4.31 per cent to RM2.7 trillion.
Assets under management in the fund management industry rose 6.9 per cent to a record RM1.14 trillion, supported by stronger fixed income returns and higher valuations.
Total funds raised through the capital market jumped 35.4 per cent to RM187.7 billion, while 60 initial public offerings were listed in 2025, a record compared with 55 in 2024.
Committed funds in the venture capital and private equity industry increased 21.66 per cent to RM30.05 billion.
Alternative financing channels continued to support small and medium enterprises (SMEs) and mid-tier companies, with RM5.7 billion raised during the year.
For 2026, SC said key deliverables under the masterplan will focus on vibrancy, inclusivity, sustainability and regional opportunities.
Initiatives rolled out so far include the MY Value Up Programme to enhance long-term value creation among listed companies, a new private debt notes framework to widen financing options for SMEs, and an expanded exchange-traded fund framework covering digital assets.
Upcoming measures include a revised Malaysian Code on Corporate Governance 2026, stronger value propositions for the Main Market and ACE Market, enhancements to the LEAP Market and digital asset exchange framework, as well as greater tokenisation of securities.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Donations gone' before reaching fire victims as 'outsiders' strip moving lorry
Article:
SANDAKAN: A lorry driver whose vehicle carrying donations for fire victims in Kampung Bahagia was stormed in a viral video said he was left shocked and saddened by the incident.
Jeffry Ayung, 36, said the incident occurred on the first day of the fire when he was delivering aid contributed by the Sungai Manila Neighbourhood Watch (KRT) and Sungai Manila mosque.
Footage of the incident showed several individuals climbing onto the moving lorry and taking items before throwing them to the ground.
Contacted today, Jeffry said the situation turned unexpectedly tense as he was heading to the designated collection point when his lorry was approached by unidentified individuals.
"I was told they were outsiders, not residents of Kampung Bahagia. I was shocked and did not expect such aggressive behaviour, but as there was no damage or injuries, we just continued driving slowly.
"By the time we reached the drop point, the donations were already gone," he said.
In Sunday's fire, an estimated 9,000 residents were affected after about 1,000 homes were destroyed in the water village settlement.
Jeffry said he felt guilty as he had been entrusted to deliver the donations from the community.
He added that it was his first time encountering such a situation after four years of volunteering to provide free transport for disaster relief efforts.
"Following the incident, we sought assistance from the authorities and non-governmental organisations (NGOs) to escort us when delivering aid.
"We delivered assistance not only to Kampung Bahagia, but also to temporary evacuation centres for the victims.
"Today is the last day we are providing the free lorry service, as we will be returning to our regular jobs," he said.
Jeffry said four of his friends had also provided free lorry transport over the past three days, delivering donations such as clothing, mineral water and dry food contributed by the public, including from nearby islands and NGOs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AmBank backs Gajahsuria Power's solar project with RM102mil loan
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: AmBank Group has extended RM102.40 million loan to Gajahsuria Power Sdn Bhd to support the development of a 29.99 megawatts alternating current (MWac) large scale solar 5 (LSS5) project in Kampung Gajah, Perak.
The loan will primarily fund working capital and project-related costs for the solar photovoltaic plant which was awarded under the LSS5 programme by the Energy Commission.
The initiative is part of the ongoing efforts to expand renewable energy capacity and reduce reliance on fossil fuels in Peninsular Malaysia.
Gajahsuria Power is a special purpose vehicle overseeing the full lifecycle of the project from developing and financing to construction, commissioning and long-term operations and maintenance.
Established in January 2025, the company is jointly owned by Sun Energy Ventures Sdn Bhd, Constant Energy Malaysia Holding Sdn Bhd (CEMH) and BayWa r.e Energy Solutions Sdn Bhd.
CEMH, the local arm of Constant Energy, will serve as the lead developer, overseeing project execution as well as future operations and maintenance.
Headquartered in Bangkok, Constant Energy has built a growing presence across Southeast Asia, with over 260 megawatts in contracted capacity and more than 120 megawatt-peak of operating solar assets in Malaysia, Thailand and Vietnam.
The group first entered the Malaysian market in 2017 through the co-development of a 5 MWac solar plant in Tawau, Sabah, under the LSS2 programme.
The facility remains operational and is regarded as one of Sabah's more advanced large-scale solar installations.
With an estimated project value of RM128 million, the Kampung Gajah solar plant is expected to begin operations by July 2027, contributing to lowering dependence on fossil fuels while supporting local economic and social development in Perak.
AmBank managing director of business banking Christopher Yap said the financing reflects the bank's continued commitment to sustainable infrastructure and low-carbon initiatives.
Constant Energy chief executive officer Frank Constant said the project showcases the company's commitment to supporting Malaysia's energy transition while expanding its renewable energy footprint across Southeast Asia.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Viral photo of children bathing in Kuantan fountain spark safety, hygiene concerns
Article:
KUANTAN: A photo of a group of children bathing in a fountain at Kuantan City Garden opposite Kompleks Teruntum has gone viral, sparking debate online.
While the image captures a moment of carefree fun amid the heat, it has also raised questions over how the children entered the water, which is designated as a non-swimming zone.
Social media users also highlighted concerns over the lack of parental supervision, while others questioned the Kuantan City Council's (MBK) monitoring of public amenities, particularly the fountain in the town centre.
The image, posted on April 20, drew mixed reactions, with some users saying such incidents were not new and that they had witnessed similar cases before.
User Insan Muslem Mukmin raised concerns over hygiene and safety, citing risks such as skin infections and potential electrical hazards involving the water pump and lighting system.
"I have been seeing this for the past month. Don't the parents feel embarrassed with people around? I really don't understand," wrote Qii Sukri.
Some users also shared posters of affordable swimming pool rates in the state capital, urging parents to take their children to more suitable locations.
Meanwhile, MBK corporate and public communications department head Hani Syazani Kamarudin Arifin said the fountain, opened in 2023, was meant as an attraction only and not for water-related activities.
"A sign reminding the public not to enter, swim, or touch the water, which was previously installed, has gone missing. We will install a new no-bathing sign soon.
"MBK will also install closed-circuit television cameras to allow direct monitoring of the fountain area, as the current cameras are mainly focused on the nearby road," he said when contacted today.
Hani Syazani said MBK will also deploy enforcement officers to conduct regular checks at Kuantan City Garden, especially during weekends.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: India orders school water bells to beat heat
Article:
NEW DELHI: Schools in India's capital must ring regular bells to remind schoolchildren to drink water as the megacity gears up to face heatwave conditions, a new city order has said.
Summer heat in the world's most populous nation can be brutal — putting millions of people at risk, with nearly 11,000 people dying due to heat stroke between 2012 and 2021, according to government data.
A heatwave in May 2024 in New Delhi saw temperatures match the capital's previous record high: 49.2C clocked in 2022.
That year was India's hottest year since thorough records began in 1901, with sizzling temperatures following a global pattern of extreme weather driven by climate change.
The temperature on Wednesday morning in New Delhi and the wider sprawling metropolitan region of 30 million residents was a relatively balmy 29.4C.
But weather forecasters predict temperatures will hit 41-43C later on Wednesday, and rise to 42-44C later in the week.
The India Meteorological Department has issued a "yellow alert" for Delhi, indicating the likelihood of a heatwave later in the week.
On Tuesday, guidelines issued by the Delhi education directorate asked schools in the city to adopt a plethora of steps to shield students.
They included avoiding "outdoor physical activities" and conducting "awareness sessions" to remind them of the importance of hydration.
"Outdoor assemblies to be curtailed or conducted in shaded/indoor areas with minimal duration," the note read. "No open air classes to be conducted."
Quirkier directions are also on offer: schools have been asked to start a "water bell" initiative to prevent dehydration and also implement a "buddy system" for students to look out for each other.
"Schools shall implement a 'Water Bell' system, whereby a bell is rung at regular intervals (every 45-60 minutes) reminding students to drink water to prevent dehydration," the circular notes.
"Each student may be paired up with another student during school hours to monitor and take care of the each other's physical wellbeing," it added. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Senior citizen jailed four months for offensive post on royal institution
Article:
SEPANG: A 64-year-old man was sentenced to four months' jail by the Sessions Court today after pleading guilty to making offensive posts involving the royal institution two years ago.
Mat Derus Ali was charged under Section 233(1)(a) of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 for consciously initiating a post with the intent to annoy others.
The offence involved a comment on an article related to the royal institution on a portal, which was viewed at MCMC Tower 1 in Cyberjaya at 12.59pm on Aug 21, 2024.
Mat Derus had posted the comment using the Facebook account Mat Derus at 2.15am on Aug 20, 2024.
Judge Ahmad Fuad Othman, in his judgment, said the offence involved sentiments that should not be touched upon, particularly regarding the Malay Rulers and the royal institution.
Deputy public prosecutor Fadhli Ab Wahab earlier urged the court to consider the public interest, arguing that the sentence must serve as a deterrent to the accused and others.
In a separate case, 52-year-old army veteran Marzuki Ismail pleaded not guilty to making offensive posts involving race, the judiciary, and the royal institution (3R) last December, using the Facebook account Marzuki Ismail.
The father of three, who served in the military for 22 years, allegedly committed the offence at 2.30pm on Dec 22, 2025. The post was viewed at 3am on Dec 24 at MCMC Tower 1 in Cyberjaya.
Fadhli offered bail at RM10,000, but defence counsel Nik Zarith Nik Moustpha pleaded for a lower amount, stating his client relies on a monthly pension of RM1,400 to support his wife and school-going children.
The court set bail at RM7,000 with one surety and fixed June 9 for mention and the submission of documents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UK agrees ban on cigarette sales for people born after 2008
Article:
LONDON: Both chambers of Britain's parliament have approved a bill that would ban children aged 17 and under from buying cigarettes during their lifetime.
The Tobacco and Vapes Bill aims to stop anyone born after Jan 1, 2009 (now aged 17) from taking up smoking.
Health minister Wes Streeting called this a "historic moment for the nation's health" that will lead to the "first smoke-free generation, protected from a lifetime of addiction and harm."
When it gets royal assent and becomes law, it will give the government powers to extend an indoor smoking ban to outdoor spaces such as children's playgrounds and areas outside schools and hospitals.
It will also give the government new powers to restrict the flavours and packaging of vapes and ban vaping in places where smoking is already banned.
The bill is part of a drive to ramp up preventative health measures and ease long-term pressure on the UK's state-funded National Health Service (NHS).
Hazel Cheeseman, director of public health charity Action on Smoking and Health (ASH), told LBC radio station that the bill was a "decisive turning point for public health."
The Labour government in June last year introduced a ban on the sale of disposable vapes, which are cheap and come in colourful packaging that make them popular with young people.
New Zealand in 2022 became the first country to enact such a law against smoking, banning the sale of cigarettes to those born after 2008. But a newly elected conservative coalition repealed the law in November 2023, less than a year after it was introduced.
The Maldives in November last year banned the sale of cigarettes to anyone born after Jan 1, 2007.
Smoking in England causes around 75,000 deaths per year and is responsible for around a quarter of all deaths, according to the NHS.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australia's online watchdog targets Roblox and Minecraft over extreme content
Article:
SYDNEY: Australia's internet watchdog raised fears on Wednesday that popular online gaming platforms such as Roblox and Minecraft were being used by "predatory adults" to target children.
The nation's eSafety Commission has sent legal notices to a host of the world's most popular gaming platforms, demanding they explain how they are working to stamp out dangerous content.
Australia is at the forefront of global efforts to protect children from online harm, enacting laws last year that ban teenagers under 16 from social media.
eSafety boss Julie Inman Grant said research had shown the vast majority of Australian children played some sort of online game.
"Predatory adults know this and target children through grooming or embedding terrorist and violent extremist narrative in gameplay," she said.
Online gaming platforms Roblox, Minecraft, Fortnite and Steam will be required to show how they are identifying and eliminating online harms, Inman Grant said.
"We've seen numerous media reports about grooming taking place on all four of these platforms, as well as terrorist and violent extremist-themed gameplay."
Inman Grant said examples included gaming platforms recreating mass shootings and World War II concentration camps.
Roblox said it had policies in place that strictly prohibited extreme content.
"We welcome engagement with eSafety on this important topic," the platform told AFP in a statement.
"We encourage anyone who sees anything concerning on Roblox to report it to us."
Australia in December banned under-16s from a raft of the world's most popular social media sites, citing the need to protect young minds from "predatory algorithms" filled with sex and violence.
Three months since the landmark laws came into effect, Australia's online safety watchdog found a "substantial proportion of Australian children" were still scrolling banned platforms. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US military says key weapons system staying in South Korea
Article:
WASHINGTON: The United States has not moved a key missile defence system out of South Korea, a US military official said Tuesday, following reports that Washington was shifting parts of it to the Middle East.
The Washington Post reported last month, citing unnamed officials, that the United States was moving parts of its Terminal High Altitude Area Defence (THAAD) system out of South Korea for use in its war with Iran.
The report had caused disquiet in South Korea, where the system is a pillar of national defence against nuclear-armed North Korea.
The commander of US forces in South Korea said Washington had "not moved any THAAD systems" out of the country.
"THAAD still remains on the peninsula currently," Xavier Brunson said at a US Senate committee hearing in Washington.
"We are sending munitions forward (to the Middle East), and those are sitting right now waiting to move," Brunson said without providing further details.
Asked if he expected the system to remain in place, Brunson replied: "We do."
THAAD is designed to intercept short, medium and intermediate-range ballistic missiles using hit-to-kill technology.
South Korean President Lee Jae Myung had said Seoul was unhappy with its reported redeployment, but acknowledged there was little the government could do about it.
Seoul's defence ministry said it could deter threats from North Korea even if the United States relocated some of its military assets.
The United States stations about 28,500 troops in South Korea, and the THAAD system was installed in South Korea in 2017.
It sparked strong protests from nearby China, which viewed the system as a threat to its national security.
North Korea is showing a "very serious increase" in its ability to produce atomic weapons, the head of the UN nuclear watchdog said during a visit to Seoul this month.
The North said this week it fired multiple short-range ballistic missiles that tested the power of its cluster munitions, the latest in a flurry of launches by the diplomatically isolated state.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Shop told to explain RM30 charge for repairing water-damaged calculator
Article:
KOTA BARU: The Kelantan Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry has issued a notice to a shop owner to explain a RM30 charge for repairing a water-damaged calculator.
The ministry's state deputy director Aswadi Jaafar said the notice was issued yesterday under the Price Control and Anti-Profiteering Act.
"The trader is required to submit complete information regarding the service charges within four working days for further investigation.
"We will examine whether there are elements of profiteering or any breaches in the transaction," he said.
Aswadi said the ministry acted following a public complaint.
The issue went viral recently after a receipt showing the RM30 repair charge was circulated on social media.
Netizens questioned the rate, describing it as disproportionate for repair work that reportedly only involved installing a used battery.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mara offers 'Peace of Mind' counselling for struggling borrowers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Majlis Amanah Rakyat (Mara) has called on its borrowers facing financial difficulties to attend a special counselling session, Peace of Mind, organised as part of a five-day carnival in conjunction with its 60th anniversary celebrations.
Mara chairman Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki said the initiative was introduced with the cost-of-living crisis in mind, with the aim of easing financial burdens.
The service is open to all Mara borrowers, including those who took loans for education as well as those who financed businesses and business premises under its entrepreneurship schemes.
"The prime minister recently said there is hardship due to the economic situation.
"So if you have problems, come and see us. There will be a special session, Peace of Mind, throughout the five-day carnival, which will run from 9am to 10pm daily," Asyraf told reporters after launching the 2026 Mara Chairman's Cup Public Speaking Competition at the Medan Mara building today.
He added that Mara would discuss solutions with borrowers, including options to restructure or reschedule repayments.
"There will be many programmes during the carnival, from small- to large-scale events. There will also be a food carnival featuring dishes from across the country, organised by well-known influencers."
The carnival, the highlight of Mara's 60th anniversary celebrations, will run from May 1 to 5 at the World Trade Centre Kuala Lumpur.
The Bumiputera agency turned 60 on March 1.
Yesterday, Mara said it had reduced loan arrears among borrowers through more aggressive measures.
"Through a more structured approach, including the expansion of auto-debit payment methods, salary deductions and e-mandates, the non-performing financing (NPF) rate was reduced from 35.2 per cent in March 2025 to 24.1 per cent in March 2026.
"Mara also introduced a more comprehensive credit management policy and strengthened the competency of its officers through professional training in credit management."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gobind demands immediate, transparent update from A-G on Durian Tunggal shooting
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: DAP national chairman Gobind Singh Deo today called on the Attorney-General to provide an immediate and transparent update on the Durian Tunggal shooting case.
He said that about five months have passed since the incident occurred, with no definitive action taken against any individuals involved.
"Despite the passage of time, there appears to be a lack of definitive action taken against any individuals involved.
"The gravity of this case cannot be overstated. Serious allegations have been made and evidence, including audio recordings, have been produced to support these claims, he said in a statement.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Saifuddin questions Muhyiddin's focus amid Middle East crisis
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Indera Mahkota Member of Parliament Datuk Seri Saifuddin Abdullah has questioned Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin's priorities, raising concerns over efforts to replace the opposition leader while the country grapples with the impact of the Middle East crisis.
In a video shared on X, Saifuddin said the opposition should be focusing on the broader implications of the conflict, particularly its potential impact on Malaysia's economy and security.
He noted that during his tenure as Opposition Leader, Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin had called for a special Dewan Rakyat sitting to deliberate on the issue.
The proposed session was aimed at addressing the fallout from the conflict involving Iran and its potential ripple effects on both the Malaysian and global economies.
Saifuddin said the ongoing crisis posed direct risks to economic stability and national security.
"What is the impact on oil prices? What is the impact on the inflation of goods? What is the impact on food security? And what is the impact on our safety, for instance, in the Straits of Malacca?" he said.
He argued that these issues should take precedence over internal political matters.
"However, Muhyiddin appears more interested in pushing for a replacement for the opposition leader," he added, questioning the timing of the move.
The debate over the transition comes after Muhyiddin recently called for a successor to be named before Parliament reconvenes in June.
The move follows Hamzah's resignation, which was confirmed by Perikatan Nasional treasurer-general Datuk Seri Ahmad Samsuri Mokhtar on April 12.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: North Korean hackers suspected of US$300mil crypto heist
Article:
SEOUL: A notorious North Korean hacking group is likely behind the theft of nearly US$300 mn in cryptocurrency over the weekend, an affected party has said, in the biggest known crypto heist this year.
It is the latest such incident linked to North Korea, whose sophisticated cybercrime programme uses stolen cryptocurrency to help fund its nuclear weapons development, according to a United Nations panel.
Digital currency news site CoinDesk said the heist on the vault of online investment tool KelpDAO on Saturday was 2026's biggest crypto exploit so far.
During the hack, two blockchain servers hosted by another crypto tech application called LayerZero were compromised, KelpDAO said Tuesday.
That allowed a cryptocurrency token linked to the major Ethereum currency to be "drained" from KelpDAO, it said.
"On Apr 18, 2026, KelpDAO was exploited for approximately US$290 mn," LayerZero said in a statement.
"Preliminary indicators suggest attribution to a highly sophisticated state actor, likely DPRK's Lazarus Group," LayerZero said, using the initials of North Korea's official name.
It assured users that "there is zero contagion to any other cross-chain assets or applications."
Blockchain technology allows transactions to do away with middlemen, including governments and banks – a concept known as decentralised finance (DeFi).
"This will make it more scary for new entrants to enter the DeFi world," said Henri Arslanian, co-founder of Nine Blocks Capital Management.
"This is clearly the job of North Korea's Lazarus group. No other group globally has the expertise and muscle power to conduct such a hack," he said Wednesday in a note.
A UN panel estimated in 2024 that North Korea had stolen more than US$3 bn in cryptocurrency since 2017.
Last year, the United States accused North Korea of being behind the theft of US$1.5 bn worth of digital assets, then the largest crypto heist in history.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mara marks 60th year with national public speaking push
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: More than 100 students from Mara education institutions have entered a national public speaking competition held in conjunction with the agency's 60th anniversary, aimed at nurturing confident and well-rounded young leaders.
A total of 102 entries were received, with 40 finalists – 25 from Maktab Rendah Sains Mara (MRSM) and 15 from Mara colleges – selected for the final.
Mara chairman Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki said the initiative reflects the agency's commitment to developing human capital beyond academic achievement.
"We want to produce a generation that is not only academically excellent, but also competitive, visionary and able to express their views confidently," he said.
The competition, themed "Mara at 60, Charting Success, Building Civilisation", offers RM10,000 in prizes.
The final will be held on April 26 at Auditorium Bandaraya here and broadcast live on Mara's official Facebook pages.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Khaled Nordin: Forest City desalination plant moved to Lok Heng as 4,000 hit by water disruption
Article:
KOTA TINGGI: More than 4,000 residents in Felda Lok Heng and Felda Waha here have been affected by prolonged water supply disruptions following a sharp drop in river levels caused by extended dry weather.
Kota Tinggi member of parliament Datuk Seri Khaled Nordin said water levels at Sungai Ulu Sedili Kecil had fallen to a critical level, rendering it unable to support operations at the Lok Heng water treatment plant.
"It has directly affected supply to both Felda settlements, impacting daily life for over 4,000 consumers," he said, adding that several mitigation efforts had been attempted.
Khaled, who is also the defence minister, said the Johor state government, Ranhill SAJ and relevant agencies had explored alternative sources, including groundwater extraction via tube wells and water transfer from Sungai Lukah.
"However, these measures could not be sustained due to limited water availability and high salinity levels in Sungai Lukah, which are unsuitable for treatment using existing facilities," he said in a statement today.
As an immediate measure, Khaled said Ranhill SAJ is relocating and installing a desalination plant from Forest City to the Lok Heng treatment facility. The unit is expected to produce up to two million litres of treated water daily.
He acknowledged slight delays in implementing the system but expressed hope it would stabilise supply as soon as possible.
In tandem, cloud seeding operations are scheduled for today and tomorrow after earlier attempts were postponed due to unfavourable cloud conditions.
"The selected dates are based on improved atmospheric conditions, which we hope will yield better results," he said.
Khaled also urged residents to remain patient as authorities intensify efforts to resolve the disruption.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Don't treat illness, disease prevention as seasonal, says deputy health minister
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Private healthcare providers are essential in the Health Ministry's shift towards upstream healthcare.
Deputy Health Minister Datuk Hanifah Hajar Taib said prevention has long been central to the ministry's mission, driven through healthy lifestyle campaigns, community-based programmes, as well as screening and awareness efforts on non-communicable diseases, mental health, infectious disease control and injury prevention.
"We are moving healthcare upstream, shifting from reactive treatment to prevention.
"With diabetes, hypertension and heart disease on the rise, your (private healthcare providers') commitment to community outreach and health education is more vital than ever.
"Prevention must not be treated as a one-off or seasonal effort. It must be embedded in everyday healthcare delivery and in how we engage communities.
"This is where public-private collaboration is crucial, extending the reach of preventive care, reinforcing national health priorities, and bringing health information and screening services closer to the people," she said at the launch of the new wing of Columbia Asia Hospital Cheras today.
Meanwhile, Hanifah Hajar said Columbia Asia has consistently emphasised early detection and advanced diagnostic technology.
She added that the expansion of Columbia Asia Hospital Cheras represents more than just an investment in infrastructure, but a long-term commitment to keeping quality care accessible.
"I encourage all Malaysians to take an active role in their health by utilising these facilities and participating in preventive programmes.
Columbia Asia Hospital Cheras regional chief executive officer Engku Marina Engku Hatim said the expansion, which increases the hospital's capacity, is a strategic move to serve rapidly growing townships, including Cheras Selatan, Balakong, Sungai Long and Kajang.
"In addition, the introduction of high-demand specialities such as neurosurgery, gynaecologic oncology, maternal-fetal medicine, hand and microsurgery, as well as robotic-assisted surgery for total knee replacement, reflects the changing health risks and care needs today.
"This expansion is not just about physical growth; it is about our commitment to the community, ensuring that we can provide timely and reliable healthcare to more families," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Back home, Hong Kong fire victim gathers memories from the ashes
Article:
HONG KONG: Dorz Cheung was at work when one of Hong Kong's deadliest fires broke out in November. Back inside his burnt home for the first time, the overwhelming feeling he had was of being "suffocated".
"Why did this tragedy happen?" Cheung, 34, said as he left the apartment, carrying some of the things most dear to his heart: his running medals, family photos, his grandmother's 50-year-old journals documenting her reflections on the Bible.
"My emotions swing between anger and sadness," he said. "When I first saw the kitchen, I was very shocked, I felt suffocated."
Authorities this week began allowing the victims of the fire - which engulfed seven residential towers, killing 168 people and eclipsing London's Grenfell Tower disaster of 2017 - to return to their homes, under government supervision, to retrieve any belongings that might have survived.
FOUND LATE GRANDFATHER'S BELONGINGS
In Cheung's room on Tuesday, a giant plush teddy bear rested on ruffled-up bedsheets.
In the living room, ash and dust covered a small piano, the humid air coming through the burned, broken windows leaving dark trails of grime on the wall.
Cheung retrieved his smartwatch, his old passport and boarding passes he had kept as reminders of his trips. To his 88-year-old grandmother's delight, he also found some of his late grandfather's belongings: documents, rings and two plaques, both inscribed with "good heart and great skills", only one of which he was able to carry out of the flat.
"After I finished packing these things up, I just stood there and looked at the apartment for a while," Cheung said.
"At that moment, I couldn't help but cry," he added. "All the mental preparation I had done beforehand seemed to have been useless."
[[nid:1423655]]
SMELL OF BURNING ON CLOTHES
Cheung had moved to that apartment after his grandfather's death during the pandemic so he could take care of his grandmother. They now live in separate 100-square-foot temporary housing units, in the same building, elsewhere in Hong Kong.
Unable to climb 14 flights of stairs, she had asked him to bring back some of her clothes. He did not take any, even though her room was the only one unaffected by the fire.
"Those clothes, when you smell them, it's still there - the smell of burning," he said.
His grandmother was on her way home when the fire broke out and alerted Cheung about it through a text message.
Cheung told her to wait at a nearby church, but she returned to the flat to cook instead.
About half an hour later, a neighbour warned her that the fire was spreading so she fled and was reunited with Cheung later that evening.
Cheung said the fire brought him and his grandmother closer together and she sometimes cooks for him at his place.
His grandmother adapted to the new place quickly and takes the bus twice a week to her old church, he said.
NEED TO FIND A WAY TO 'STAND BACK UP'
But he still struggles sometimes, he said. In December, he was exhausted and emotionally drained, with no appetite, feeling nauseous, tense and sweaty. After finding out he could return to his home, he began to have nightmares again.
Still, he hopes the experience can make him stronger.
"After losing my home, nothing else can really defeat me, because this was already the hardest thing to deal with."
He has resumed running marathons to release tension.
"Life must go on. When something major happens, after you've cried and broken down, you really have to find a way to stand back up," Cheung said.
"If you don't look after yourself, if you don't care for yourself, how can you take care of others, or go on to do other things?"
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: WFH a short-term buffer, not structural fix — economist
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Work-from-home (WFH) arrangements should be viewed as a short-term buffer rather than a sustainable macroeconomic solution to Malaysia's fuel and fiscal challenges, according to an economist.
The Centre for Market Education chief executive officer, Carmelo Ferlito, said the government's push towards remote working is likely aimed at "buying time" amid ongoing global uncertainty, rather than addressing structural issues.
While fewer commuters on the road could lead to marginal fuel savings, Ferlito said such reductions would not be enough to offset the rising cost of subsidies driven by global oil price volatility.
"From my perspective, the move that has been adopted now is just a move that tries to buy some time to see where the conflict is going, if it is going to end fast or not," he said in an interview with Bernama World recently.
Ferlito said that policymakers must first be clear about their objective, whether WFH is meant to reduce subsidy burdens or to prepare for potential fuel supply shortages, as each scenario requires a different policy response.
He also highlighted concerns that widespread remote working could disrupt business dynamics, particularly in industries that rely on physical collaboration and in-person interaction to drive innovation.
Instead of enforcing blanket measures, Ferlito recommended a more decentralised approach, allowing businesses to determine their own working arrangements based on operational needs and energy considerations.
"Firms are in a better position to decide how to balance efficiency and energy use. Flexibility and clear communication from the government are key," he said.
In addition, he proposed structural reforms to the subsidy system, including separating market-driven fuel pricing from targeted income support for vulnerable groups.
"I have proposed a system of targeted and proposed vouchers specifically designed for the poor and specifically designed for transportation systems.
"But the core idea, I think, should be to separate the market price, which should be a market-determined price for oil, from initiatives that are oriented toward helping the poor with income support," he said.
Looking ahead, Ferlito said clear communication and constant engagement between the government and business community should be a priority.
"So for me, the best signal to be sent there is clarity on the available supply. I understand it's not easy because the scenario, as we have seen in these hours, is constantly evolving.
"But clear communication on this and constant engagement with the business community and avoiding a solution that fits everybody, applied from the top down to everybody, will be the way forward," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mother of hit-and-run victim appeals for driver to come forward
Article:
KUCHING: A 68-year-old mother is appealing for the driver involved in a March hit-and-run incident that cost her only son his left leg to come forward and assist police investigations.
Afidah Suhaili said her family harbours no resentment and only hopes the individual will take responsibility for the accident.
"There is CCTV footage from a nearby house showing the driver getting out of the car before driving off, but the registration number could not be clearly identified.
"We are not seeking revenge. We only hope the person will come forward so the matter can be resolved properly," she told reporters at her home in Jalan Bako.
She also urged anyone with dashcam or CCTV footage of the crash to contact the police.
In the 8.30pm incident on March 30, food delivery rider Putra Aqmal Syukrie Sabli, 24, was travelling from Jalan Song towards Stutong when a vehicle, believed to be a Toyota Avanza, suddenly turned into a junction.
Putra Aqmal said he tried to avoid the vehicle, but his left leg struck the side of the car, throwing him onto the road.
"I shouted for help and remained conscious. I waited about an hour before the ambulance arrived," said Putra Aqmal, who had recently returned to work after the Aidilfitri break.
Afidah said that while her son's leg was not severed at the scene, he waited nearly 15 hours for surgery, which began at 4.30am the following day.
Doctors initially attempted to reattach blood vessels but eventually performed an amputation to save his life.
Putra Aqmal, a former tahfiz student at Madrasah Darul Uloom Islamiyyah Kuching and former acting imam at Surau Mahmudi in Kampung Gita, said he is coming to terms with his condition.
"Allah has not taken my life, only my leg," he said. He previously earned about RM2,000 a month as a rider and also taught Quranic recitation to children.
Afidah said her son is undergoing rehabilitation and is expected to receive a prosthetic leg worth RM50,000 in the coming months.
"We are submitting assistance forms to the Social Welfare Department. There have also been contributions from an elected representative and the public," she said, noting that monthly medical expenses are estimated at RM1,000.
Kuching police chief Assistant Commissioner Alexson Naga Chabu said the case is being investigated under Section 43(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for reckless and dangerous driving.
"No police report has been lodged by the driver, and no witnesses have come forward so far," he said. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM230mil zakat misappropriation: Two men remanded for four days [WATCH]
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Two men, including the deputy chairman of a non-governmental organisation, have been remanded for four days to assist in investigations into the alleged misappropriation of RM230 million in zakat funds.
The remand order against the suspects, aged 57 and 61, was granted by the Magistrate's Court here following an application by the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC).
Bernama reported that the duo, who were arrested in the Klang Valley yesterday, arrived at the court complex at 9.07am in an MACC van.
A third person in his 50s was also arrested yesterday at the Selangor MACC office.
Investigators have
seized
18 luxury vehicles, including a Porsche, Mercedes-Benz, Range Rover, and Honda Civic Type R, as well as houses and other properties estimated to be worth RM11 million.
Other confiscated assets include cash and branded watches.
Additionally, 33 bank accounts containing approximately RM120 million have been frozen as part of the probe.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cost hikes may force 30 pct of padi farmers to skip planting season
Article:
ALOR STAR: About 30 per cent of padi farmers nationwide, including those in Sabah and Sarawak, are expected to skip the new planting season due to rising operational costs.
Malaysian Padi Farmers Association (Pesawah) chairman Abdul Rashid Yob said of the estimated 200,000 farmers across the country, many were struggling with high costs, particularly following diesel price hikes.
He said some farmers had chosen to halt operations temporarily while others had pivoted to alternative crops such as corn, vegetables, or oil palm.
"In this 30 per cent estimate, it includes farmers who are taking a break, switching crops, or converting plots into fish ponds. There are also those planting padi only for personal consumption.
"For instance, a farmer with one hectare might only plant a quarter for self-sustenance. Given the 600,000 hectares of padi fields nationwide, the impact on total production will be significant," he said when contacted today.
Rashid added that the sharp spike in costs had left many unable to afford machinery hire and market-rate diesel.
"Ploughing costs have increased by over 50 per cent, squeezing the income of already struggling farmers. When diesel prices fluctuate weekly, farmers and machinery operators cannot set stable wages, disrupting the entire production chain. Both parties are feeling the pinch," he said.
He noted that the RM300 per hectare Padi Ploughing Incentive (IPKP) remained insufficient to cover actual field costs.
:The aid does not solve the problem because machinery operators still purchase non-subsidised diesel, with the resulting high costs eventually passed down to the farmers.
"We hope the government includes padi agricultural machinery in the Subsidised Diesel Control System (SKDS) so farmers can enjoy stable prices. Additionally, the government should consider raising the floor price of padi to RM3,000 per tonne as a long-term solution.
"If the floor price is not reviewed, farmers will continue to face losses. We need comprehensive measures, not just short-term incentives," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Social Welfare Dept denies role in child placement, says arrangement was private
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Social Welfare Department has refuted viral allegations that they had requested an individual to take over the care of a child, clarifying that the arrangement was a private agreement between the child's mother and the individual.
In a statement issued today, the department described the claims circulating on social media as "inaccurate" and warned that they could cause public confusion over legal child placement procedures.
They said that, based on their records, no official placement was made by the department in 2020, as alleged.
"The care of the child was a private arrangement between the biological mother and the individual, conducted without the Social Welfare Department's involvement," the statement read.
The department said they only became formally involved in the case on April 29, 2022, when the Kuala Lumpur Federal Territory office received a referral to provide advisory services on the child's custody status and documentation.
Following the referral, a Social Welfare Department protector conducted an investigation and assessment in line with the "best interests of the child" principle under the Child Act 2001 (Act 611).
The matter was subsequently formalised in court later that year.
"On Aug 3, 2022, the Court for Children issued an order under Section 30 of Act 611, granting custody to the individual until the child turns 18.
"This order was made after taking into account a statutory declaration of consent from the biological mother, and the mutual agreement between all parties involved," the statement added.
The department urged the public to refrain from speculation, warning that such claims could lead to misunderstandings about its role and potentially jeopardise the child's wellbeing.
They reiterated their commitment to handling all cases with professionalism and integrity, with the welfare of children remaining their top priority under the law.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia seen as safe-haven energy investment hub – MBSB
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is emerging as a key investment destination in Southeast Asia (SEA), as capital shifts towards regions capable of delivering immediate or near-term energy production to offset potential supply disruptions, MBSB Investment Bank Bhd said.
In a research note, it said recent offshore discoveries by Murphy Oil in Vietnam, alongside Malaysia's positioning as a relatively stable and investment-friendly market, have reinforced the region's strategic importance.
"As a result of this structural shift in global energy investment flows, the Malaysia Bid Round 2026 was officially launched by Petroliam Nasional Bhd under the theme 'Advantaged Energy: Accelerating and Shaping Tomorrow'.
"The initiative represents a strategic effort to position Malaysia as a preferred 'safe haven' investment destination, capitalising on the ongoing reallocation of capital away from higher-risk regions," it said.
MBSB said the offering is structured to appeal to a broad spectrum of investors, combining lower-risk mature assets with higher-impact frontier exploration opportunities.
It said the offering includes nine exploration blocks across Malaysia, spanning high-impact frontier areas such as the Sandakan Basin, high-potential emerging areas such as the West Sarawak Basin, and near-field mature areas such as the Malay Basin.
In addition, discovered resource opportunities comprise ready-to-develop clusters in Peninsular Malaysia, Sarawak and Sabah.
"In addition, six discovered resource opportunities are being offered, providing ready-to-develop pathways for monetisation.
"These assets are supported by extensive subsurface data and technical insights, enabling investors and solution providers to accelerate development timelines while reducing exploration risk," it said.
MBSB said Malaysia is targeting annual upstream investments of RM50 billion to RM60 billion to sustain momentum in the sector, ensuring a steady pipeline of exploration and development activities to support long-term production sustainability and energy security.
"We maintain a 'positive' call on the energy sector, underpinned by structurally tighter global supply conditions and elevated geopolitical risk premiums following the escalation of tensions in the Middle East.
"The sharp re-rating in crude oil prices, with Brent sustaining above the US$100 per barrel level, reflects not demand strength but persistent supply-side disruptions, particularly from the Strait of Hormuz and broader regional instability," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Guarding sovereignty in the Strait of Malacca
Article:
THE Strait of Malacca is a critical international shipping lane heavily traversed by merchant vessels to and from numerous nations to locations worldwide.
Of increasing interest to the US government, particularly during the current administration, involving Iranian tankers transiting the waterway.
It is therefore imperative that Indonesia, Malaysia, and Singapore cooperate more effectively in preserving the sovereignty of nations that transit through the waterway, ensuring global marine transportation security, and upholding the law of the sea, particularly those laws applicable to straits used for international navigation.
International law generally is applied through the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), particularly in Part III that pertains to the regulation of the transit passage, including restrictions and privileges through international straits by coastal states.
In this context, coastal states have full sovereignty over their territorial seas; however, they are required to ensure continuous transit to foreign vessels through international straits to use the sea lane, the sea floor and the atmosphere above the surface of the sea, as well as to allow for the flying over of the strait.
From the perspective of a so-called user state, the US view of international law in relation to the Strait of Malacca has recently attracted considerable attention.
In statements made by the Trump administration in relation to the Strait of Malacca, particular emphasis was placed on the right to freedom of navigation, on the one hand, and on the prevention of a single actor from claiming dominance and control at strategic maritime locations, on the other.
The report that the United States military would pursue Iranian oil tankers in the vicinity of the Malacca Strait further exemplifies how major global rivalries are playing out in Southeast Asia's maritime domain.
Washington's hardening line on sanctions enforcement against North Korea may pose legal and political problems for the US in areas where it is seeking to enforce compliance among its allies and friends.
Indonesia, Malaysia and Singapore are the stakeholders and actors responsible for ensuring safety at sea in the busy Strait. Their interests in ensuring the safety and security of sea lanes, which are likely to increase as trade volumes grow, are economic, political and legal.
These interests could be undermined by external powers claiming that a greater military presence at and from sea is needed to protect their shipping.
All three states are parties to UNCLOS and, accordingly, their responses to what they perceive as growing safety concerns at sea in the Strait must be coordinated and not merely reactive.
Existing cooperative efforts such as the Malacca Strait Patrols (MSP) – a trilateral cooperative effort among Malaysia, Indonesia and Singapore to patrol the Malacca Strait in order to prevent piracy and armed robbery at sea – have generally proven effective.
However, as more powers become increasingly engaged in the Indian Ocean, the present cooperative frameworks are unlikely to be sufficient to mitigate risk for operators.
Current cooperative frameworks face several inherent weaknesses that would likely be severely tested by the great power rivalries now emerging.
Additionally, limitations in the sharing of intelligence, inconsistencies in applicable law, and divergent approaches to the management of operational crises are all areas of potential risk.
The MSP agreement does not extend to foreign military action conducted against third-country-flagged vessels, and therefore exposes operators to further risk, both legal and operational.
The coming challenges will largely be ones of planning, first operationally and then strategically.
There is a real possibility that external actors will try to frame the issue of the Strait of Hormuz as a matter of global security, in need of an international military intervention.
From Iran's perspective, an attack on an Iranian-owned or controlled oil tanker would require turning over command of littoral state forces to an external actor.
From the perspective of the littoral states, there are significant internal imbalances in terms of naval capabilities and strategic visions that increase the likelihood that each country could be drawn into separate arrangements with external actors.
Finally, while there are technological challenges to enhancing awareness of maritime traffic through the Straits, there are also serious threats to navigation by cyber-attack, which will continue to test the cooperative arrangements in place.
We also run the risk of misinterpreting interactions with other navies from different countries. Strategic thinking is therefore needed.
Indonesia, Malaysia and Singapore have to immediately establish a real-time intelligence sharing mechanism and harmonise domestic laws and regulations concerning the enforcement of maritime zones.
Diplomatically, they must speak with one voice. In discussions about the management of the Malacca Strait and the transit of ships through the waterway, they must project an image of management in accordance with international law and current regional mechanisms.
In seeking outside cooperation, the process must be open, on an even playing field, and consistent with a shared vision as to the future of the Strait as a major maritime transportation hub and an owner-driven process that supports the principles embedded in UNCLOS.
Capacity building should be a one-time operation and should not turn into a permanent security presence in the littoral.
With increasing attention being paid to the Strait of Malacca by actors around the world, Indonesia, Malaysia and Singapore have a unique opportunity to strengthen their trilateral cooperation on issues affecting the strategic Strait and reaffirm their regional leadership on issues related to the Strait and maritime security more broadly.
This cooperation, rooted in a commitment to laws and norms, is essential to safeguarding sovereignty, protecting a critical global waterway, and reinforcing the importance of effective regional governance in the Indo-Pacific.
* The writer is a retired Indonesian diplomat
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Business administration graduate finds greener pastures in dairy farming
Article:
SLIM RIVER: After graduating with a Bachelor of Business Administration from Universiti Putra Malaysia 24 years ago, K. Christol Paul knew an office job was not for him.
The Slim River native chose instead to become his own boss by venturing into dairy farming.
Now 52, Christol runs a five-hectare farm with 70 Friesian Sahiwal dairy cows imported from Australia and New Zealand, alongside 120 goats.
He began his journey with just six dairy cows taken over from his brother, David.
Although he initially lacked experience, guidance from David and courses organised by the Muallim Veterinary Services Department helped him become a successful breeder.
"Success in dairy farming is not easy. It requires resilience and spirit to ensure a sustainable livelihood," he said, adding that the sector depends entirely on one's own effort.
Christol, who is part of a pilot dairy farming project under the Tapah Dairy Industry Research Centre, thanked the department for its support.
"Through this project, I received 10 Friesian Sahiwal cows from Australia and a Napier grass grinding machine four years ago," he said.
Christol begins his day at 6am, milking the animals with the help of three workers. All livestock are fed Napier grass grown on his farm.
"Livestock farming is profitable. Besides milk, we can sell the animals, and even the manure is in high demand as fertiliser," he said.
He added that he remains eager to give back to society by sharing his knowledge and helping those in need.
The Perak Sejahtera 2030 initiative continues to empower the livestock sector through aid, the development of agrifood hubs, and modern farming projects to ensure food security.
The initiative aligns with Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Saarani Mohamad's vision of establishing Perak as the country's food basket to drive state progress.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Burst pipe triggers water disruption in JB, Kota Tinggi and Pontian
Article:
JOHOR BARU: A burst pipe at the Sungai Johor water treatment plant disrupted supply to vast areas across the state capital, Kota Tinggi and Pontian today, affecting residential, industrial and key public facilities.
In a statement, Ranhill SAJ said the incident forced the postponement of scheduled maintenance at the plant, resulting in an unplanned disruption across multiple zones.
Repair works at the site have since been completed, with water restoration being carried out in stages.
Among the worst-hit areas are Tiram, Skudai, Iskandar Puteri and parts of Kota Tinggi, involving entire townships, industrial hubs and high-density residential zones.
Key locations affected include major industrial estates in Tiram, commercial centres in Tampoi Indah, and critical facilities such as hospitals, schools and data centres in Iskandar Puteri.
Large-scale residential developments, including Nusa Bestari, Bukit Indah and Horizon Hills, are also experiencing supply interruptions, while rural settlements and felda schemes in Kota Tinggi have not been spared.
The disruption is expected to impact daily routines and business operations, particularly in manufacturing and service sectors dependent on continuous supply.
Ranhill SAJ said updates would be provided periodically as restoration progresses and the system stabilises.
"Recovery will be implemented in stages until supply returns to normal," SAJ said, adding that it regretted the inconvenience caused.
Consumers may channel enquiries or complaints via the utility's hotline at 1800 887474 or official communication platforms, including www.ranhillsaj.com.my or customer.care@ranhill.com.my
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: YTL Cement issues offer document for Concrete Engineering takeover offer
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: YTL Cement Bhd today issued the offer document in relation to its unconditional mandatory takeover offer for the remaining shares it does not own in Concrete Engineering Products Bhd, giving the first closing date of the offer as May 13 at 5pm.
The cash offer of RM2.60 per share follows its acquisition of 53.49 per cent equity interest in the producer of prestressed spun concrete piles and poles for RM103.79 million.
The offer has therefore been extended to the remaining 46.51 per cent stake, representing 34.71 million shares not already owned by YTL Cement and persons acting in concert.
YTL Cement said the acquisition and subsequent offer are in line with its strategy to expand its precast concrete manufacturing segment and strengthen vertical integration across its cement, aggregates and construction materials businesses.
"The precast concrete manufacturing sector is expected to accelerate, driven by increasing demand for precast concrete products due to higher productivity, faster delivery and better efficiencies in the construction of public infrastructures, residential housing and industrial buildings including data centres," it said.
YTL Cement also said it intends to maintain Concrete Engineering's listing status on the Main Market of Bursa Malaysia.
Concrete Engineering shares rose three sen to RM2.63 at 10.56am, with 42,000 shares traded.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM5b MSME aid only ‘small win,’ more needed to tackle crisis, says Guan Eng
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 22 — DAP advisor Lim Guan Eng has described the government’s latest RM5 billion aid package for small businesses as a “small win,” urging Putrajaya to implement bolder measures like making the first RM50,000 of all loans interest-free and collateral-free.
The Bagan MP was responding to Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim’s announcement of several measures to support micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs), including the new loan facility and a one-year delay for e-invoicing implementation.
While welcoming the move, Lim argued that for the aid to be a “big win,” more direct financial relief is needed.
“I urge the prime minister to approve the first RM 50,000 portion of the entire loan be deemed interest-free and collateral free.
If the loan is for RM 180,000, then interest should be charged and collateral imposed only on the remainder RM 130,000 of the loan.
He also said there should also be a moratorium of interest payment made by MSMEs on existing loans.
According to Lim, these measures are critical to address the immediate cash crunch faced by businesses as suppliers increasingly refuse credit terms and demand cash payments.
He also dismissed other government relief, such as temporary tax exemptions for reimported goods, as “minimal.”
Instead, he urged for a suspension of the expanded Sales and Service Tax (SST) and the 2 per cent EPF contribution for foreign workers.
The government’s new loan guarantee facility also raises financing coverage to 80 per cent and extends repayment periods to 10 years.

Title: Ikram suspends three members caught in RM230m ‘zakat’ fund scandal
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 22 — Pertubuhan Ikram Malaysia (Ikram) has suspended three members arrested by the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) in connection with a massive investigation into the alleged misappropriation of approximately RM230 million in
zakat
funds.
The move comes after two individuals, including the deputy chairman of a non-governmental organisation (NGO), were remanded for four days starting today to assist in the MACC probe.
In a statement today, Ikram president Badlishah Sham Baharin said that the organisation views the matter seriously and is extending its full cooperation to the MACC.
“As an administrative measure and without prejudice to any party, the individuals involved have been immediately relieved of all positions and responsibilities within the organisation,” Badlishah said.
“This step has been taken to ensure the smooth running and transparency of the investigation process.”
He stressed that the case involves the actions of individuals and does not directly implicate the organisation as a whole.
However, he gave his assurance that if the members are found guilty, firm disciplinary action will be taken to uphold the integrity of IKRAM.
“Ikram fully respects the investigation process currently being carried out by the MACC and will continue to extend all appropriate cooperation,” he added.
The organisation stated it will not comment further on the matter to respect the ongoing legal process and has urged the public to give authorities the space to conduct their investigation.
Yesterday, the Selangor MACC arrested three men in an operation around the Klang Valley.
According to a source, two men in their 50s and 60s were arrested in the afternoon, while another man in his 50s was detained when he appeared at the Selangor MACC office to give a statement.
According to media reports, MACC also seized 18 luxury vehicles, including a Porsche, Mercedes, Range Rover, and Honda Civic Type R, as well as houses and other properties estimated to be worth about RM11 million.
The case is being investigated under the MACC Act 2009.
Established in 2009, Ikram is a
dakwah
(Islamic outreach),
tarbiah
(Islamic education), and welfare organisation operating in line with the teachings and way of life of Islam.

Title: RM230m zakat funds probe: Two suspects, including NGO deputy chairman, remanded for four days over alleged misappropriation
Article:
SHAH ALAM, April 22 — Two individuals, including the deputy chairman of a non-governmental organisation (NGO), have been remanded for four days, from today, to assist in investigations into the alleged misappropriation of about RM230 million in
zakat
funds.
The remand order against the two men, aged 57 and 61, was issued by Magistrate Ameera Mastura Khamis after the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) submitted an application at the Magistrate's Court here today.
The case is being investigated under the MACC Act 2009.
Earlier, the two individuals arrived at the Shah Alam Court complex at 9.07am in a MACC vehicle.
Yesterday, the Selangor MACC arrested three individuals, including a deputy chairman of an NGO, on suspicion of misappropriating about RM230 million in
zakat
funds.
According to a source, two men in their 50s and 60s were arrested yesterday afternoon in an operation around the Klang Valley, while another man in his 50s was detained at about 2pm when he turned up to give a statement at the Selangor MACC office. — Bernama

Title: Exco: Selangor considering seawater desalination for water security
Article:
SHAH ALAM, April 22 — The Selangor government is studying the feasibility of a seawater desalination plant to ensure the sustainability of clean water supply in the state, said Infrastructure and Agriculture Committee chairman Datuk Izham Hashim.
He said the construction of the infrastructure could increase clean water production capacity by about 300 million litres per day (MLD) for use in the state.
“Two sites have already been proposed, but a final decision will be made based on technical, strategic and cost considerations, including its overall business viability,” he said at the Selangor state assembly sitting today.
He was responding to a supplementary question from R. Rajiv (PH-Bukit Gasing), who wanted to know the feasibility of the infrastructure as well as its impact on service costs and water tariffs if implemented.
Izham said Selangor’s current water reserve margin stands at around 17.4 per cent, or 1,130 MLD, while total clean water production capacity from all 34 water treatment plants (LRAs) is 6,500 MLD, with average actual demand at 5,370 MLD.
He said Pengurusan Air Selangor Sdn Bhd (Air Selangor) also expects the required annual water reserve capacity to increase by about 20 per cent over the next five years.
“Selangor’s water reserve is classified as high based on the reserve margin guidelines set by the National Water Services Commission (SPAN), which is above 15 per cent,” he said.
“Four LRAs that are under construction or in the pipeline are the Rasau LRA Phase 1, with a capacity of 700 MLD, expected to be completed in early 2027; Rasau LRA Phase 2 (700 MLD) in 2031; Labohan Dagang LRA Phase 2 (200 MLD) in 2029; and Langat 2 LRA Phase 2 (760 MLD), which is expected to be completed in 2030,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Johor man charged with bribing police RM18,500 to ignore friends’ criminal histories
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, April 22 — A 31-year-old factory supervisor pleaded not guilty at the Sessions Court here today to a charge of bribing a police officer with RM18,500 to allegedly overlook his and his friends' criminal records.
The accused, M. Dhivan, made the plea after the charge was read to him in Bahasa Melayu before Judge Datuk Che Wan Zaidi Che Wan Ibrahim.
According to the charge sheet, Dhivan is accused of corruptly giving RM18,500 in cash to Sergeant Koshaziza Idris of the Segamat district police headquarters' patrol unit.
The bribe was allegedly an inducement for the officer to not take legal action against Dhivan and his friends, who reportedly have records related to criminal activities and drug possession.
The offence was allegedly committed in January last year at Kilometer 1.89 of Jalan Buloh Kasap-Segamat.
The charge was framed under Section 17(b) of the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) Act 2009 for offering a bribe.
If convicted under Section 24(1) of the same Act, he faces a maximum prison sentence of 20 years and a fine of not less than five times the bribe amount or RM10,000, whichever is higher.
The prosecution, led by MACC prosecuting officer Fazal Lisam Abidin, argued for a high bail amount to serve as a public deterrent.
However, defence counsel K. Nahtan pleaded for a lower sum. He stated that his client, a father of two, earns only RM2,000 a month as a factory supervisor and supports his unemployed wife and young children.
Judge Che Wan Zaidi set bail at RM6,000 with one surety and ordered the accused to report to the Ipoh MACC office monthly.
The court fixed June 5 for the next case mention and for the submission of documents.

Title: Federal Court dismisses challenge against whipping as cruel and inhumane
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, April 22 — The Federal Court today upheld the constitutionality of whipping, ruling in a split decision that the punitive measure does not violate the fundamental liberties guaranteed by the Federal Constitution.
Chief Justice Datuk Seri Wan Ahmad Farid Wan Salleh, delivering the majority judgment, dismissed the argument that the sentence was cruel, inhumane, or discriminatory.
He affirmed that the punishment does not breach Article 5, which guarantees the right to life and personal liberty, or Article 8, which ensures equality before the law, the
New Straits Times
reported.
The ruling stemmed from a legal challenge brought by three inmates who sought to have their whipping sentences set aside.
They argued through a review application that the punishment carries a risk of death and is arbitrary, thus violating their constitutional rights.
However, the nation’s top court distinguished whipping from a death sentence, stating that its purpose is punitive, not to deprive a person of their right to life.
“By their very purpose, penalties are designed to be punitive,” the chief justice said in the ruling.
“To isolate whipping as uniquely cruel or degrading is to ignore the reality that all forms of lawful punishment, to some degree, infringe upon the personal dignity and comfort of the individual.”
MORE TO COME

Title: Mother pleads for hit-and-run Avanza driver to come forward after son loses leg in Kuching
Article:
KUCHING, April 22 — A mother is appealing for the driver involved in a hit-and-run incident in late March that left her only son with his left leg amputated to come forward and lodge a police report to assist with investigations.
Afidah Suhaili, 68, said her family harbours no resentment and only hopes the individual will take responsibility for the incident.
“There is CCTV footage from a nearby house. The driver can be seen getting out of the car, then driving off. The vehicle registration number could not be clearly identified.
“We are not seeking revenge, but only hope the person will come forward and take responsibility so the matter can be resolved properly,” she told reporters when met at her home in Jalan Bako recently.
She also urged anyone with CCTV or dashcam footage of the incident to assist with police investigations.
In the 8.30 pm incident on March 30, Putra Aqmal Syukrie Sabli, 24, who works as a food delivery rider, was travelling from Jalan Song to Stutong near here when a vehicle believed to be a Toyota Avanza suddenly turned into a junction.
According to Putra Aqmal, he tried to avoid the vehicle but his left leg brushed against the side of the car, causing him to fall onto the road.
“I shouted for help and was still conscious. I waited about an hour before the ambulance arrived,” he said, adding that he had just returned to work after the Aidilfitri break.
Afidah said that although her son’s leg was not severed at the scene, he had to wait nearly 15 hours before undergoing surgery, which began at about 4.30 am the following day.
Doctors initially attempted to reattach the blood vessels in his leg, but later decided that amputation was necessary to save his life, she said.
Putra Aqmal, a former tahfiz student at Madrasah Darul Uloom Islamiyyah Kuching and former acting imam at Surau Mahmudi in Kampung Gita, said he is coming to terms with what has happened.
“Allah has not taken my life, only my leg,” he said, adding that he previously earned about RM2,000 a month as a delivery rider and also taught children to recite the Quran.
Afidah said her son is still undergoing follow-up treatment and rehabilitation, and is expected to receive assistance for a prosthetic leg worth RM50,000 in the coming months.
“We are in the process of submitting assistance forms to the Social Welfare Department. There have also been contributions from an elected representatives and members of the public,” she said, adding that monthly expenses for medication and other needs are estimated at around RM1,000.
Meanwhile, Kuching Police Chief ACP Alexson Naga Chabu said the case is being investigated under Section 43(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for reckless and dangerous driving.
“No police report has been lodged by the driver involved to date, and no witnesses have come forward so far to assist with investigations,” he told Bernama when contacted.
Members of the public wishing to assist the family may channel contributions to a CIMB Bank account 7634990678 under the name Aqma Syaidatulfieka Sabli (the victim’s sister). — Bernama

Title: Selangor accelerates greening campaign after planting eight million trees since 2022
Article:
SHAH ALAM, April 22 — The Selangor government is on track to achieve its goal of planting 11 million trees, having already surpassed eight million since its state-wide greening campaign began, the state assembly was told today.
State Public Health and Environment Committee chairman Jamaliah Jamaluddin confirmed that the initiative, launched in May 2022, has recorded a total of 8,050,011 trees planted across the state.
Speaking during the question-and-answer session, Jamaliah attributed the success to a comprehensive strategy involving all local authorities, district offices, and collaborations with public and private agencies.
“It also encourages participation from schools, non-governmental organisations and corporations through corporate social responsibility programmes to plant trees in open spaces as part of efforts to green the environment,” she said.
The campaign is not limited to just reforestation efforts, but also covers urban areas, riverbanks, and coastal zones, Jamaliah said.
To ensure accurate tracking, the Selangor Forestry Department (JPNS) is collaborating with the Selangor Climate Change Adaptation Centre (SCAC) to consolidate planting data from all participants, including universities and schools.
A diverse range of species has been planted, including native forest trees like ramin, meranti tembaga, merbau, and kelat paya, while local councils have focused on ornamental and landscaping trees such as bunga tanjung, tecoma, and bungor.
New target set for 2026
To maintain momentum, Jamaliah announced a new target for 2026, calling on each local authority and government agency to plant approximately 100,000 trees throughout the year.
“This is in line with the decree of the Sultan of Selangor for the state to plant no fewer than 11 million trees,” she said.
Jamaliah added that both JPNS and local councils are responsible for the ongoing monitoring and maintenance of the planted trees. The forestry department will focus on high-risk areas like permanent forest reserves, while local councils will manage urban greenery in line with the National Landscape Guidelines 2.0.

Title: Actor Ako Mustapha alleges son bullied by five students; police confirm report
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — Police have confirmed receiving a report regarding the alleged bullying of actor Ako Mustapha’s 10-year-old son, Mikael Iskandar, by five other students at a school here.
Wangsa Maju District Police Chief Assistant Commissioner Annas Sulaiman verified that his agency has been notified,
Berita Harian
reported.
“I confirm that the Wangsa Maju District Police Headquarters (IPD) has received a police report as stated. As of now, no investigation paper has been opened,” he said in a brief statement today.
The issue came to public attention after Ako Mustapha alleged that his son had been brutally bullied.
The actor said he discovered the incident after his son returned from school with visible bruises on his body, which his son alleged to be from a beating by two seniors and three classmates.
The actor said that he immediately sent photos and videos of the injuries to the class teacher.
His son was subsequently examined at a government clinic before being referred to Kuala Lumpur Hospital (HKL) for a check-up.

Title: Sandakan fire witness says sorry after video of firefighter abuse goes viral
Article:
SANDAKAN, April 22 — A man has apologised to the Fire and Rescue Department for using abusive language in a viral video recorded during the recent fire at Kampung Bahagia here.
Rozaizar Rozainie, 39, said his actions were driven by heightened emotions and stress as he witnessed the chaotic scene, including children panicking as the flames spread.
“I was overwhelmed with emotion at the time after seeing children running around, unsure of where to go as the fire spread.
